content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance        Administration              Executive/Legal              Enforcement              Conflict of Interest
(916) 222-5562            322-5560                  322-5901                  322-6441                322-6444

July 9, 1979

Robert O. Franks
Deputy City Attorney
100 No. Claudina St., Suite 607
Anaheim, California 92805

Dear Mr. Franks:

Thank you for your letter of March 30, 1979, on behalf of
E. Llewellyn Overholt, Jr., regarding the financial disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Specifically, you asked
whether salary and reimbursement for expenses which Councilman
Overholt has received in return for the performance of services
as an Inheritance Tax Referee are reportable on his Statement
of Economic Interests. The facts as I understand them are as
follows.

Inheritance Tax Referees are public officials who are
appointed by the State Controller pursuant to Revenue and Taxation Code Section 14771. Duties of referees are performed under
the direction of the superior court and include appraising
estates which are undergoing probate and estimating taxes which
are due on property transferred to individuals sharing in the
estate. Revenue and Taxation Code Sections 14772 and 14773
and Probate Code Section 609 provide that referees shall be
compensated by the estate on the basis of a fixed percentage
of the value of the estate they appraise and reimbursement for
actual and necessary expenses. In addition, referees are paid
a fee fixed by the court for tax estimates which they prepare
with respect to property which is transferred to individuals
sharing in the estate.

Government Code Section 82030 defines income as that term
is used in the Political Reform Act. Subsection (b)(2) specifically exempts salary and reimbursement for expenses from a state
or local government agency from the definition of income. As

Robert O. Franks
July 9, 1979                                                    Page Two


stated above, tax referees are paid a fee by the estate which is
fixed by statute. These payments are made to referees in their
capacity as public officials for services performed under the
direction of the courts. Because the referee may only perform
appraisals at the direction of the court, and in some instances
the Controller's Office, and the amount to be paid the referee
is fixed by statute, we conclude that payments received by the
referee for performing appraisals constitute salary and reimburse-
ment for expenses from a government agency. Accordingly, these
payments are not income within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act and need not be reported on Councilman Overholt's
Statement of Economic Interests.

     If you have further questions, please do not hesitate to
contact our office.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight Dickerson
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DD:ma

CITY OF ANAHEIM, CALIFORNIA
106 North Claudina Street, Suite 607
Anaheim, California 92805

Office of
CITY ATTORNEY

Telephone:
714/533-5361

March 30, 1979

Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Conflict of Interest Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 307
Sacramento, California 95804

Subject: Disclosure Requirements with Respect to Income
Received by an Inheritance Tax Referee

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

Pursuant to our telephone conversations of March 28 and 30, 1979,
with respect to the subject matter, this office requests advice
in connection with income disclosure requirements, if any, of a
city councilman who has earned income as an inheritance tax
referee.

Such advice should take into consideration the following:

1. The State Controller is required to appoint at least
one person in each county as an inheritance tax referee for the
county (Revenue and Taxation Code Section 14771).

2. We have been informed that an inheritance tax referee
is employed by the Superior Court, not the State Controller
though; the Controller is given the initial power to appoint and
remove a referee (R & T 14771) and the power to approve the fees
payable to him (R & T 14772).

3. The person appointed to the position of inheritance
tax referee need not be an attorney.

4. The duties of the inheritance tax referee include appraisal and reporting on the character and probable value of all
properties in a decedent's estate.

5. An inheritance tax referee serves the court in the administration of the estate and the State Controller and, to some
extent, the court in the administration of the inheritance tax
law.

MR. DWIGHT DICKERSON
March 30, 1979                                                Page 2

6. The compensation of referees is fixed by statutes at
certain percentages based on the value of the estate. In addition to the fixed fee, they are entitled to actual and necessary
expenses to be allowed by the court. (R & T 14772 and 14773 and
Probate Code 609).

7. Fees for services performed by an inheritance tax
referee are paid out of the funds of two estates. In some instances, the fees are advanced by the attorney handling the probate matter.

In your response, it is requested that the following questions,
along with others you deem appropriate, be addressed:

1. Is the income earned as an inheritance tax referee
reportable in a case where the referee is also a city councilman.

2. If so, how should the source of the income be reported?

3. Are the services rendered as an inheritance tax referee
considered to be legal services pursuant to Schedule 721-V?

4. If so, who is the client? If the fee is $1,000.00 or
more from an estate, what source should be listed (i.e., the decedent's estate, the survivor of a joint tenancy, etc.)?

This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of March 30,
1979, wherein I informed you that Councilman E. Llewellyn
Overholt Jr.'s Statement of Economic Interests will reflect his
income received as an inheritance tax referee on Schedule 721-D.
Since an inheritance tax referee does not have to be an attorney,
we have opined that such services are not legal services within
the purview of Schedule 721-E. If the FPPC determines otherwise,
upon notification to this office, Councilman Overholt will be advised to amend his Statement in accordance with such determination.

In the event you need more information with respect to this
matter, please give me a call at 714/539-5301.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM F. HOPKINS, CITY ATTORNEY

By [illegible] ROBERT O. FRANKE
Deputy City Attorney

WPH:ROF:fm

cc: Councilman Overholt
    Linda Roberts, City Clerk

sections.question: Is the income earned as an inheritance tax referee reportable in a case where the referee is also a city councilman? If so, how should the source of the income be reported? Are the services rendered as an inheritance tax referee considered to be legal services pursuant to Schedule 721-F? If so, who is the client? If the fee is $1,000.00 or more from an estate, what source should be listed?
sections.conclusion: Because the referee may only perform appraisals at the direction of the court, and in some instances the Controller's Office, and the amount to be paid the referee is fixed by statute, we conclude that payments received by the referee for performing appraisals constitute salary and reimbursement for expenses from a government agency. Accordingly, these payments are not income within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and need not be reported on Councilman Overholt's Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether salary and reimbursement for expenses received by a city councilman for performing duties as an Inheritance Tax Referee are reportable as income on his Statement of Economic Interests under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Payments received by an Inheritance Tax Referee for performing appraisals constitute salary and reimbursement for expenses from a government agency under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2), and therefore are exempt from reporting as income on the Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (City Attorney letter dated March 30, 1979) and FPPC response (July 9, 1979). Response is clearly structured with facts, legal analysis, and conclusion. Confidence is high as all key sections are present and clearly articulated.

QUESTION: Is the income earned as an inheritance tax referee reportable in a case where the referee is also a city councilman? If so, how should the source of the income be reported? Are the services rendered as an inheritance tax referee considered to be legal services pursuant to Schedule 721-F? If so, who is the client? If the fee is $1,000.00 or more from an estate, what source should be listed?

CONCLUSION: Because the referee may only perform appraisals at the direction of the court, and in some instances the Controller's Office, and the amount to be paid the referee is fixed by statute, we conclude that payments received by the referee for performing appraisals constitute salary and reimbursement for expenses from a government agency. Accordingly, these payments are not income within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and need not be reported on Councilman Overholt's Statement of Economic Interests.

embedding.summary: The FPPC determined that salary and reimbursements received by City Councilman E. Llewellyn Overholt Jr. as an Inheritance Tax Referee are exempt from disclosure requirements because they constitute government agency compensation under the Political Reform Act.
